subject: ap-calculus-bc
theme: Parametric, polar, and vector-valued functions extend calculus beyond y = f(x) — enabling analysis of curves defined by separate component functions or radius-angle relationships.

core_concepts.title: Parametric Derivatives
core_concepts.detail: For $x = f(t)$, $y = g(t)$: the slope is $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$. Horizontal tangent when $\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$ (and $\frac{dx}{dt} \neq 0$). Vertical tangent when $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$ (and $\frac{dy}{dt} \neq 0$).

core_concepts.title: Parametric Second Derivative
core_concepts.detail: $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{\frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{dy}{dx}\right]}{\frac{dx}{dt}}$. First compute $\frac{dy}{dx}$ as a function of $t$, differentiate that with respect to $t$, then divide by $\frac{dx}{dt}$.

core_concepts.title: Parametric Arc Length and Speed
core_concepts.detail: Arc length: $L = \int_a^b \sqrt{(x')^2 + (y')^2}\,dt$. Speed at time $t$: $\sqrt{(x')^2 + (y')^2}$. Total distance traveled equals the arc length integral.

core_concepts.title: Polar Area
core_concepts.detail: Area enclosed by $r = f(\theta)$ from $\alpha$ to $\beta$: $A = \frac{1}{2}\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} [r(\theta)]^2\,d\theta$. For area between two polar curves, subtract inner from outer.

core_concepts.title: Polar Slope
core_concepts.detail: Since $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$: $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{r'\sin\theta + r\cos\theta}{r'\cos\theta - r\sin\theta}$. Use Product Rule on $x$ and $y$.

core_concepts.title: Vector-Valued Functions
core_concepts.detail: Position: $\mathbf{r}(t) = \langle x(t), y(t)\rangle$. Velocity: $\mathbf{v}(t) = \langle x'(t), y'(t)\rangle$. Acceleration: $\mathbf{a}(t) = \langle x''(t), y''(t)\rangle$. Speed: $|\mathbf{v}(t)|$.

formulas.name: Parametric Slope
formulas.description: The slope of a parametric curve. Divide the rate of change of y by the rate of change of x, both with respect to the parameter t.

formulas.name: Parametric Arc Length
formulas.description: Length of a parametric curve from t = a to t = b. The integrand is the speed, so arc length = total distance traveled.
formulas.example: For $x = \cos t$, $y = \sin t$ on $[0, 2\pi]$: $L = \int_0^{2\pi}\sqrt{\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t}\,dt = 2\pi$.

formulas.name: Polar Area
formulas.description: Area swept by a polar curve from angle α to β. The 1/2 comes from the area formula for a thin triangular sector.

formulas.name: Speed (Parametric/Vector)
formulas.description: The magnitude of the velocity vector. Always non-negative. Equals the rate at which arc length accumulates.
formulas.example: For $\mathbf{r}(t) = \langle 3t, 4t\rangle$: speed $= \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$ (constant speed along a line).

exam_tip: For polar area, always determine the correct bounds $\alpha$ and $\beta$ by setting $r = 0$ or graphing the curve — wrong bounds are the most common error. For parametric second derivatives, do NOT just take $\frac{d^2y/dt^2}{d^2x/dt^2}$.
